description: Table 1A dialog: Five Card Draw, Hank (California Condor), Phase 1. Includes pattern reveal, Hank checklist, gambler's fallacy coaching, conceptual and transfer checklists, and gate-passed sequence.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: This is the simplest version of poker there is. Five cards. You decide how many to trade in. Best hand wins.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Bets here are fixed — five seeds to open, five seeds to call. Keeps it clean while you're learning the hands.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: That's Hank. California Condor. He's been a member here longer than I have. Doesn't mean he knows what he's doing at the table.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Here — take this. Hand rankings. Best hand at the top, worst at the bottom. Keep it open if you want.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Let's play.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Hank reached into his jacket. He's still in.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Here in the front room, guests of the club always bet first.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: First hand. Just play it. Watch what Hank does.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Watch Hank.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Keep an eye on what Hank does.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Every seed you put in the pot matters. Think about why before you commit.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Standing pat. You like what you have.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Starting fresh. Alright.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Good hand.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Hank paid for that one.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Hank had it. Happens.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Think about what Hank did differently that hand.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Folded. Sometimes that's the right move.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Good fold. Most people don't.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: You've played a few hands with Hank now. You may be noticing a few things.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: He bets every hand. Calls everything. Doesn't matter what's in his cards — he's in, every time.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: That's useful. A player who never folds tells you exactly what to do when you have a strong hand.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: What have you noticed about how Hank plays?

options.text: He bets on every single hand.
He raises when he has strong cards.
He never folds, no matter what.
He folds when his cards are weak.
His bets stay the same regardless of his cards.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: That's exactly it. Bets every hand, never folds, same no matter what's in his cards. Once you know that, you know everything you need to play against him.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Not quite. Look at what he does every time — without exception.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Almost. {{needToCheck}} more to check, {{needToUncheck}} to uncheck.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Here's what I see: he bets every hand, never folds, and his cards don't change how he plays. Every time. That's the whole picture.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Now — one more thing Hank shows you. He keeps losing, but he always thinks he's about to turn it around.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Some players think: 'Five bad hands in a row — I must be due for a good one.' Feels true. But the deck doesn't know what just happened.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Every shuffle resets everything. Fifty-two cards, same odds as always. Nothing gets 'owed' to you.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Why doesn't a bad streak make a good hand more likely next time? Check everything that's true.

options.text: The deck is reshuffled every hand — it has no memory of what came before.
Each deal is independent. Past results don't affect the cards.
Good hands are 'stored up' and have to come out eventually.
You're more likely to get a strong hand after several weak ones.
The probability of any particular hand is the same every time the deck is shuffled.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Right. The deck has no memory. Every shuffle — same 52 cards, same odds. Nothing carries over.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Think about what happens to the deck between hands. Does anything carry over from the last deal?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Almost. {{needToCheck}} more to check, {{needToUncheck}} to uncheck. Focus on what the shuffle does.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: The shuffle resets everything. The deck has no record of previous hands. Each deal is completely independent — nothing is stored up, nothing is owed.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Same idea, different situation. Stay with me.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: I have a bag: 3 brown seeds, 7 green. I draw one, note the color, put it back, shake the bag. I do this five times — every draw comes up green. What's true about the sixth draw? Check all that apply.

options.text: Green is still more likely — there are more green seeds in the bag.
Brown is 'overdue' — it must come up soon.
The chance of drawing brown is still exactly 3 out of 10, same as always.
The bag doesn't know what color came up last time.
Getting green five times means the green seeds are running low.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Exactly. The seed goes back in. The bag resets. Same odds every time — the bag has no memory, same as the deck.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: The seed goes back in after each draw. What does that mean for what's in the bag next time?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Almost. {{needToCheck}} more to check, {{needToUncheck}} to uncheck. Focus on what 'put it back and shake' means for the odds.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: With replacement: the bag resets after every draw. The odds stay at 3 out of 10 brown and 7 out of 10 green — every time, regardless of what came before.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: You've got it. The deck has no memory. The bag has no memory. Probability describes what can happen — not what's 'owed.'

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Table 1A was the foundation. You've seen what Hank is doing, and you understand why a bad streak doesn't change the next hand.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: There's another table in The Front Room. Lucky's there — a Rock Pigeon who thinks he's always due for a comeback. You're ready for him.

nodes.speaker: hank
nodes.text: I'm in.

nodes.speaker: hank
nodes.text: Bet.

nodes.speaker: hank
nodes.text: Raise.

nodes.speaker: hank
nodes.text: I'm good.

nodes.speaker: hank
nodes.text: One.

nodes.speaker: hank
nodes.text: Two.

nodes.speaker: hank
nodes.text: Three.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Hank has bet on every single hand you've seen. If he plays 5 more hands while you're at this table — how many times do you expect him to bet?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Exactly. Five bets in five hands. Not 'probably five' — definitely five. When you know someone's pattern that well, you don't predict what they'll do. You know.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: He can't bet more than once per hand. Five hands — five is the ceiling.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Has Hank skipped a single hand yet? Think about what you've actually seen.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Five. He bets every hand — no exceptions. Once you see that pattern clearly, you can plan around it.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Three folds in a row. Folding bad cards is smart — that's exactly right. Just make sure it's the cards driving it, not the last result. The deck has no memory of what you just went through.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Three losses in a row. That happens. But remember — the deck doesn't know that. Next hand is a completely fresh deal.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: You've been swapping a lot of cards. Chasing a stronger hand is a real strategy — just know that trading four or five rarely pays off the way you hope. What are you usually holding when you do that?
